sections.heading: Interpretation
sections.title: Asset declaration for individuals and family members
sections.summary: This section requires individuals to disclose their assets and those of their spouse and minor children, including immovable property, motor vehicles, partnerships, securities, cash, jewellery, and other assets exceeding 50,000 rupees. The disclosure covers both assets within Mauritius and outside the country. The declaration must be completed with specific details for each asset category.

requirements.text: Disclose immovable property (freehold and leasehold) in Mauritius and outside Mauritius
Disclose motor vehicles
Disclose interest in partnerships, societies, joint ventures, or succession
Disclose securities including treasury bills and units
Disclose cash in bank
Disclose cash in hand exceeding 50,000 rupees
Disclose jewellery and precious metals
Disclose other assets exceeding 50,000 rupees in aggregate (specify)

sections.source_quote: My assets and those of my spouse and minor children (extent and nature of interests therein) in Mauritius and outside Mauritius are as follows - (a) immovable property – (i) freehold …………………………………………………………………………. (ii) leasehold ……………………………………………………………………….. (b) motor vehicles ………………………………………………………………………….. (c) interest in any partnership, société, joint venture or succession …………………. (d) securities including treasury bills, units, etc. ……………………………………….. (e) cash in bank …………………………………………………………………………… (f) cash in hand exceeding 50,000 rupees ……………………………………………….. (g) jewellery and precious metals ………………………………………………………. (h) other assets exceeding 50,000 rupees in the aggregate (specify) ………………
sections.relevance_reason: Pure asset declaration, no data protection/privacy focus

sections.heading: Dissemination of information by Director
sections.title: FIU Information Dissemination and Reporting
sections.summary: The Director must disseminate information to relevant authorities when suspicious transactions are suspected. Reporting persons or auditors must provide information within 15 working days if requested. Failure to comply results in fines up to one million rupees and imprisonment for up to five years. The FIU also has exemptions from registration duties for documents it signs.
sections.dtia_summary: Details information dissemination protocols and reporting obligations critical for DTIA compliance in cross-border financial intelligence sharing and data handling.

requirements.text: Where there are grounds to suspect money laundering, predicate offences or terrorism financing, the Director shall disseminate information and the results of the analysis of the FIU to the relevant investigatory authority, supervisory authority, overseas financial intelligence unit, the Counterterrorism Unit, Registrars or comparable body for appropriate action.

Where a report of a suspicious transaction is made under section 14, the Director may request further information in relation to the suspicious transaction from the reporting person or auditor who made the report and a reporting person or auditor who is, or appears to be, involved in the transaction.

Where the FIU becomes aware of information which gives rise to a reasonable suspicion that a money laundering offence or terrorism financing offence might have been committed or is about to be committed, or a request for information is made by any investigatory or supervisory authority, the Director may request further information from any reporting person or auditor involved in the transaction.

Where a reporting person or auditor receives a request for information under subsection (2) or (3), the reporting person or auditor shall furnish FIU with the requested information promptly but not later than 15 working days after the request.

Where a report of a suspicious transaction is made under section 14, the Director shall require the reporting person or auditor to keep the records in respect of that suspicious transaction for such period as may be specified in the notice.

The Director may request a reporting person or auditor to inform him whether a person is or has been a client of the reporting person or auditor, acting on behalf of any client, or a client acting on behalf of another person.
A reporting person or auditor shall comply with the request made under subsection (6) within such time as may be specified in the request.

Any reporting person or auditor, or any director, employee, agent or legal representative of a reporting person or auditor who fails to supply information requested by FIU by the date specified in the request or falsifies, conceals, destroys or otherwise disposes of information, shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and imprisonment for a term not exceeding 5 years.

sections.source_quote: Dissemination of information by Director (1) Where there are grounds to suspect money laundering, predicate offences or terrorism financing, the Director shall disseminate information and the results of the analysis of the FIU to the relevant investigatory authority, supervisory authority, overseas financial intelligence unit, the Counterterrorism Unit, Registrars or comparable body for appropriate action.
sections.relevance_reason: Money laundering reporting obligations

sections.heading: Reporting of suspicious transaction by reporting person or auditor
sections.title: Suspicious Transaction Reporting Obligations
sections.summary: Reporting persons and auditors must report suspicious transactions to the FIU within 5 working days of becoming aware of them. Failure to report within this timeframe results in fines up to one million rupees and imprisonment for up to five years. The FIU provides feedback on reporting obligations but emphasizes that this does not replace internal screening mechanisms.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets mandatory reporting timelines for suspicious transactions, critical for DTIA compliance in cross-border financial intelligence operations.

requirements.text: Every reporting person or auditor shall, as soon as he becomes aware of a suspicious transaction, make a report to FIU of such transaction promptly but not later than 5 working days after the suspicion arose.
FIU shall provide feedback to reporting persons and relevant supervisory authorities in relation to the obligations specified in subsection (1).
A feedback under subsection (1A) shall be of a general nature and shall not be construed to be a substitute for the reporting person's own internal screening mechanisms.
For the purpose of subsection (1), the burden of reporting a suspicious transaction to FIU shall, in the case of a credit union, be on the internal controller of the credit union.

Where a reporting person or auditor becomes aware of a suspicious transaction or ought reasonably to have become aware of a suspicious transaction and fails to make a report to FIU not later than 5 working days after the suspicion arose, he shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and imprisonment for a term not exceeding 5 years.

sections.source_quote: Reporting of suspicious transaction by reporting person or auditor (1) Notwithstanding section 300 of the Criminal Code and any other enactment, every reporting person or auditor shall, as soon as he becomes aware of a suspicious transaction, make a report to FIU of such transaction promptly but not later than 5 working days after the suspicion arose.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data reporting obligations with penalties

sections.heading: Risk assessment
sections.title: Risk assessment and customer due diligence requirements
sections.summary: Reporting persons must conduct risk assessments for money laundering and terrorism financing. They must identify and assess risks related to customers, countries, products, services, transactions, and geographic areas. Risk assessments must consider business size, product complexity, customer relationships, third-party reliance, and technological developments. Reporting persons must document assessments and implement policies to mitigate identified risks. Enhanced due diligence is required for high-risk customers or transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires comprehensive risk assessments for money laundering and terrorism financing, impacting data transfer and processing across borders. Mandates enhanced due diligence for high-risk transactions and customers, affecting data sharing and storage practices.

requirements.text: Reporting persons must identify, assess and understand money laundering and terrorism financing risks for customers, countries, geographic areas, products, services, transactions, or delivery channels
Reporting persons must consider all relevant risk factors before determining risk levels and mitigation measures
Risk assessments must be appropriate for the business size and nature
Risk assessments must take into account all relevant risk factors including business activities, products, customers, technological developments, and national risk assessments
Reporting persons must identify and assess money laundering or terrorism financing risks for new products, business practices, or technologies
Reporting persons must document risk assessments in writing and keep them up to date
Reporting persons must establish policies, controls, and procedures to mitigate identified risks
Reporting persons must monitor, review, and update policies, controls, and procedures
Reporting persons must maintain written records of policies, controls, procedures, and changes
Reporting persons must maintain written records of communication of policies, controls, and procedures
Reporting persons must not establish or maintain anonymous or fictitious accounts
Reporting persons must undertake customer due diligence measures for new accounts, transactions above 500,000 rupees, wire transfers, or suspected money laundering
Reporting persons must apply enhanced due diligence for high-risk customers
Reporting persons may apply simplified due diligence for low-risk customers
Reporting persons must apply CDD measures for virtual asset service providers for transactions above 1,000 USD
Reporting persons must record virtual asset wallet addresses for transactions below 1,000 USD
Reporting persons must apply CDD measures for customers not physically present using reliable digital identification
Reporting persons must apply CDD measures based on risk assessment outcomes
Reporting persons must maintain records of customer transactions for 7 years
Reporting persons must maintain records of suspicious transaction reports for 7 years

sections.source_quote: Every reporting person shall take appropriate steps to identify, assess and understand the money laundering and terrorism financing risks for customers, countries or geographic areas and products, services, transactions or delivery channels; consider all relevant risk factors before determining what is the level of overall risk and the appropriate level and type of mitigation to be applied.
sections.relevance_reason: Money laundering risk assessment for financial data

sections.heading: Offences relating to obligation to report and keep records and to disclosure of information
sections.title: Offences for failing to report, destroy records or disclose information
sections.summary: Reporting persons and their directors/employees must report breaches of reporting obligations under sections 17-17G, maintain records, and prevent false transactions. Failure to comply results in fines up to 10 million rupees and imprisonment up to 5 years. Similarly, persons who falsify information relevant to mutual assistance requests or disclose investigations into money laundering offences face fines up to 1 million rupees and imprisonment up to 5 years.
sections.dtia_summary: Fails to report, destroy records or disclose information: imposes penalties for non-compliance with reporting obligations and record-keeping, relevant to data transfer impact assessments involving financial data.

requirements.text: Reporting persons must report breaches of reporting obligations under sections 17-17G
Reporting persons must maintain records
Reporting persons must prevent false transactions
Failure to comply with reporting obligations results in fines up to 10 million rupees and imprisonment up to 5 years
Falsifying information relevant to mutual assistance requests is an offence
Disclosing investigations into money laundering offences is an offence
Failure to comply with information falsification or disclosure results in fines up to 1 million rupees and imprisonment up to 5 years

sections.source_quote: Any reporting person, or any director, employee, agent or other legal representative of a reporting person who, knowingly or without reasonable excuse – (a) fails to comply with section 17, 17A, 17B. 17C, 17D, 17E, 17F or 17G; (b) destroys or removes any record, register or document which is required under this Act or any regulations; or (c) facilitates or permits the performance under a false identity of any transaction falling within this Part, shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding 10 million rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 5 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Fines/imprisonment for breach reporting

sections.heading: Provision of information by FIU to investigatory authorities, supervisory authorities,
sections.title: FIU sharing information with supervisory authorities and investigatory bodies
sections.summary: The FIU must share information with supervisory authorities, Counterterrorism Units, Real Estate Agent Authorities, Financial Reporting Councils, or Registrars if it relates to potential money laundering, corruption, or investigations. The FIU must also share information with investigatory authorities within 48 hours if relevant to ongoing investigations. Confidentiality agreements with external bodies may require consent before sharing information. The FIU must respond to requests for suspicious transaction reports within 21 days, with possible extensions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates timely sharing of sensitive financial data with authorities, requiring DTIAs to assess data flows to supervisory bodies and investigatory authorities while ensuring confidentiality agreements are respected.

requirements.text: FIU may share information with supervisory authorities if it relates to their functions, Counterterrorism Unit, Real Estate Agent Authority, Financial Reporting Council, or Registrars
FIU must share information with investigatory authorities within 48 hours if relevant to investigations
FIU must share information with the Financial Crimes Commission for possible corruption offences
FIU may provide suspicious transaction reports to supervisory authorities for compliance purposes
FIU must respond to requests for suspicious transaction reports within 21 days, with possible 21-day extension
Information shared from outside Mauritius must have consent from the originating body

sections.source_quote: Where it becomes aware of any information, which- (a) may be relevant to the functions of any of the supervisory authorities, the Counterterrorism Unit, the Real Estate Agent Authority, the Financial Reporting Council or Registrars; and (b) does not of itself justify a dissemination to any of the investigatory authorities under section 13, FIU may, by itself or shall, at the request of the supervisory authorities the Counterterrorism Unit, the Real Estate Authority or the Financial Reporting Council or Registrars, subject to subsection (4), pass on the information to the relevant supervisory authority the Counterterrorism Unit the Real Estate Agent Authority, the Financial Reporting Council or Registrar.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data sharing for financial crime

sections.heading: Provision of information by supervisory authorities, Counterterrorism Unit, Real Estate Agent
sections.title: Supervisory authorities sharing information with FIU for money laundering
sections.summary: Supervisory authorities, Counterterrorism Units, Real Estate Agent Authorities, Financial Reporting Councils, and Registrars must immediately share information suggesting money laundering or suspicious transactions with the FIU. No liability exists for disclosures made under this section, and the term 'officer' includes directors, employees, agents, and legal representatives.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires immediate sharing of financial data with the FIU for money laundering investigations, necessitating DTIAs to evaluate data flows between supervisory bodies and the FIU while ensuring no liability for disclosures.

requirements.text: Supervisory authorities, Counterterrorism Units, Real Estate Agent Authorities, Financial Reporting Councils, and Registrars must share information suggesting money laundering or suspicious transactions with FIU immediately
No liability exists for disclosures made under this section by supervisory authorities or their officers
The term 'officer' includes directors, employees, agents, and legal representatives

sections.source_quote: Notwithstanding any other enactment, where, at any time in the course of the discharge of its functions, any supervisory authority, the Counterterrorism Unit, the Real Estate Authority, the Financial Reporting Council or Registrar receives, or otherwise becomes aware of, any information suggesting the possibility of a money laundering offence or suspicious transaction, the supervisory authority, the Counterterrorism Unit, the Real Estate Authority, the Financial Reporting Council or Registrar, shall, forthwith pass on that information to the FIU.
sections.relevance_reason: Money laundering data sharing with FIU

sections.heading: Money laundering offence to be extraditable
sections.title: FIU accounting and reporting requirements
sections.summary: The FIU must maintain proper accounting records for all transactions, submit a 3-year strategic plan, financial estimates, and an annual report compliant with IPSAS and corporate governance standards. The Director is responsible for timely submission of reports to the Board and Minister, with specific deadlines for each report type. Disciplinary actions may be taken for non-compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes detailed reporting and record-keeping requirements for the FIU, impacting DTIAs through data storage and retention obligations for financial records and compliance documentation.

requirements.text: FIU must keep proper accounting records for all transactions
FIU must submit a 3-year strategic plan to the Minister by 30 June each year
FIU must submit financial estimates to the Minister by 31 August each year
FIU must prepare an annual report including financial statements, activities report, and governance report
Director must submit annual report to Board within 3 months after financial year ends
Director must submit annual report to Director of Audit within 30 April after financial year ends
Director of Audit must submit annual report and audit report to Board within 6 months
Board may take disciplinary action for officers who fail to comply with reporting requirements

sections.source_quote: The FIU shall keep and maintain proper accounting records for the purpose of recording all transactions relating to its undertakings, funds, activities and property.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial reporting and record-keeping for FIU

sections.heading: Commencement
sections.title: Commencement of the Act
sections.summary: This section specifies the commencement date of the Financial Intelligence and Anti-Money Laundering Act. It details the effective dates of the Act's provisions, including the establishment of the Financial Intelligence Unit and related regulatory frameworks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section sets the effective date for the Act, which impacts data transfer assessments by determining when new obligations for financial intelligence and anti-money laundering activities come into force.

requirements.text: The Act commences on the date specified in the First Schedule

sections.source_quote: 37. Commencement
sections.relevance_reason: commencement date irrelevant to data compliance

sections.heading: Short title
sections.title: Professional accountants under Financial Reporting Act
sections.summary: Professional accountants and public accountants under the Financial Reporting Act are defined as sole practitioners, partners, or employed professionals within member firms. This section specifies the scope of professionals covered by the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Clarifies which professionals are subject to financial intelligence requirements, ensuring accurate identification of entities needing compliance.

requirements.text: Professional accountants and public accountants under Financial Reporting Act are sole practitioners, partners, or employed professionals within member firms

sections.source_quote: Professional accountant, and public accountant under the Financial Reporting Act only where they are sole practitioners, partners or employed professionals within member firms
sections.relevance_reason: Pure definition of financial professionals, no data compliance relevance

sections.title: Interpretation of key terms in the Act
sections.summary: This section defines critical terms used throughout the Act, including 'AML/CFT', 'bank', 'FIU', 'suspicious transaction', and 'reporting person'. These definitions establish the scope and meaning of obligations for financial institutions and reporting entities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides essential definitions that impact data transfer assessments by clarifying terms like 'suspicious transaction' and 'reporting person', which are critical for determining when data transfers require special safeguards.

requirements.text: AML/CFT means anti-money laundering and combatting the financing of terrorism and proliferation
Bank includes non-bank deposit taking institutions licensed under the Banking Act
FIU means the Financial Intelligence Unit established under section 9 (1)
Suspicious transaction means a transaction that gives rise to reasonable suspicion of money laundering or terrorism financing
Reporting person means a bank, financial institution, cash dealer or member of a relevant profession or occupation

sections.source_quote: 2002. Interpretation
sections.relevance_reason: Defines critical terms for financial data compliance

sections.heading: 2. (cont.)
sections.title: Continuation of key terms in the Act
sections.summary: This section continues defining terms used in the Act, including 'proliferation', 'property', 'real estate agent authority', and 'supervisory authorities'. These definitions are essential for understanding the scope of obligations under the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides additional definitions critical for data transfer assessments, particularly regarding 'suspicious transaction' and 'reporting person', which determine when data transfers require special safeguards.

requirements.text: Proliferation means the manufacture, production, possession, acquisition, stockpiling, storage, development, transportation, sale, supply, transfer, import, export, transshipment or use of nuclear, chemical, or biological weapons
Property has the same meaning as in the Financial Crimes Commission Act 2023
Real Estate Agent Authority means the Real Estate Agent Authority established under section 3 of the Real Estate Agent Authority Act 2
Supervisory authorities include the Bank of Mauritius, Financial Services Commission, and Registrar of Co-operative Societies

sections.relevance_reason: Definitions of key terms for data compliance

sections.heading: Establishment of the FIU
sections.title: Establishment of Financial Intelligence Unit
sections.summary: This section establishes the Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) with authority to administer and discharge functions under the Act. The Director, appointed by the President on Prime Minister's recommendation with consultation from the Leader of the Opposition, must act without fear or favour and is only subject to the President in disciplinary matters. The Director oversees FIU administration and may appoint assistants.
sections.dtia_summary: Establishes the FIU as a central authority for financial intelligence with governance structure relevant to cross-border data sharing and financial crime investigations.

requirements.text: There is established for the purposes of this Act a Financial Intelligence Unit which shall have all the powers necessary to administer, and discharge its functions under, this Act.

The head of the FIU shall be the Director who shall be a person of high repute with substantial experience in the financial services industry or law enforcement and experience in management and accounting and appointed by the President on the recommendation of the Prime Minister made in consultation with the Leader of the Opposition, on such terms and conditions as the Prime Minister may determine.
The Director shall be responsible for the administration and management of the FIU and shall be assisted by such persons as may be appointed by the Director to assist him.

In the discharge of his functions and the exercise of his powers under this Act, the Director shall act without fear or favour and, subject to section 12, shall not be subject to the direction or control of any other person or authority other than, in matters of discipline, the President acting on the advice of the Prime Minister.

sections.source_quote: Establishment of the FIU (1) There is established for the purposes of this Act a Financial Intelligence Unit which shall have all the powers necessary to administer, and discharge its functions under, this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Governance and record-keeping for financial data

sections.heading: Functions of the FIU
sections.title: FIU Functions for Financial Intelligence
sections.summary: The FIU acts as the central agency for receiving, analyzing, and disseminating information related to suspected money laundering, terrorism financing, and predicate offenses. It collects and processes information from relevant enactments, issues guidelines to professionals and auditors, and exchanges information with overseas financial intelligence units. The FIU also conducts research on money laundering causes and consequences.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines the FIU's role in cross-border financial intelligence sharing and data exchange with international bodies, critical for DTIA compliance in financial crime investigations.

requirements.text: The FIU shall be the central agency in Mauritius responsible for receiving, requesting, analysing and disseminating to the investigatory and supervisory authorities, the Counterterrorism Unit and Registrars disclosures of information - (a) concerning suspected proceeds of crime and alleged money laundering offences; (b) required by or under any enactment in order to counter money laundering; or (c) concerning the financing of any activities or transactions related to terrorism.
For the purposes of this Act, the FI
The FIU shall issue guidelines to members of a relevant profession or occupation falling under its purview on measures to combat money laundering, financing of terrorism and proliferation.

The FIU shall issue guidelines to auditors, reporting persons and internal controllers of credit unions as to the manner in which a report under section 14 shall be made and additional information may be supplied to FIU on a suspicious transaction.
The FIU shall exchange information with overseas financial intelligence units and comparable bodies.
The FIU shall undertake, and assist in, research projects in order to identify the causes of money laundering and terrorist financing and its consequences.

sections.source_quote: Functions of the FIU (1) The FIU shall be the central agency in Mauritius responsible for receiving, requesting, analysing and disseminating to the investigatory and supervisory authorities, the Counterterrorism Unit and Registrars disclosures of information - (a) concerning suspected proceeds of crime and alleged money laundering offences; (b) required by or under any enactment in order to counter money laundering; or (c) concerning the financing of any activities or transactions related to terrorism.
sections.relevance_reason: FIU functions for financial intelligence and cross-border data sharing

sections.heading: Exercise of functions of the FIU
sections.title: FIU Function Execution Process
sections.summary: The FIU's functions are executed by the Director or appointed personnel. The Director must consult with law enforcement, government agencies, and reporting persons or auditors to further the FIU's objectives in combating money laundering. This section ensures coordination with relevant entities for effective financial intelligence operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies how the FIU executes its functions, including coordination with authorities, which impacts data sharing and cross-border financial intelligence operations.

requirements.text: The functions of the FIU shall be exercised by the Director or such of the persons appointed under section 9(3) as the Director may determine.

In furtherance of the functions of the FIU, the Director shall consult with and seek such assistance from such persons in Mauritius concerned with combating money laundering, including law officers, the Police and other Government agencies and reporting persons or auditors, as the FIU considers desirable.

sections.source_quote: Exercise of functions of the FIU (1) The functions of the FIU shall be exercised by the Director or such of the persons appointed under section 9(3) as the Director may determine.
sections.relevance_reason: FIU coordination for financial intelligence

sections.heading: The Board
sections.title: Company Service Providers
sections.summary: Company Service Providers are defined as registrars of companies. This section specifies the scope of entities that may be subject to financial intelligence requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Identifies company service providers as entities that may require financial intelligence monitoring, particularly in relation to company formation and management.

requirements.text: Company Service Provider is defined as Registrar of Companies

sections.source_quote: Company Service Provider Registrar of Companies
sections.relevance_reason: Defines entities subject to financial intelligence requirements

sections.heading: Reporting of suspicious transaction by reporting person or auditor
sections.title: Suspicious Transaction Reporting Obligations
sections.summary: Reporting persons and auditors must report suspicious transactions to the FIU within 5 working days of becoming aware of them. Failure to report within this timeframe results in fines up to one million rupees and imprisonment for up to five years. The FIU provides feedback on reporting obligations but emphasizes that this does not replace internal screening mechanisms.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets mandatory reporting timelines for suspicious transactions, critical for DTIA compliance in cross-border financial intelligence operations.

requirements.text: Every reporting person or auditor shall, as soon as he becomes aware of a suspicious transaction, make a report to FIU of such transaction promptly but not later than 5 working days after the suspicion arose.
FIU shall provide feedback to reporting persons and relevant supervisory authorities in relation to the obligations specified in subsection (1).
A feedback under subsection (1A) shall be of a general nature and shall not be construed to be a substitute for the reporting person's own internal screening mechanisms.
For the purpose of subsection (1), the burden of reporting a suspicious transaction to FIU shall, in the case of a credit union, be on the internal controller of the credit union.

Where a reporting person or auditor becomes aware of a suspicious transaction or ought reasonably to have become aware of a suspicious transaction and fails to make a report to FIU not later than 5 working days after the suspicion arose, he shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and imprisonment for a term not exceeding 5 years.

sections.source_quote: Reporting of suspicious transaction by reporting person or auditor (1) Notwithstanding section 300 of the Criminal Code and any other enactment, every reporting person or auditor shall, as soon as he becomes aware of a suspicious transaction, make a report to FIU of such transaction promptly but not later than 5 working days after the suspicion arose.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data reporting obligations with penalties

sections.heading: Lodging of reports of suspicious transactions
sections.title: Lodging of suspicious transaction reports to FIU
sections.summary: Reporting persons and auditors must submit suspicious transaction reports to the Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) in approved formats. Reports must include transaction details, suspect identification, business relationships, and impact on financial stability. No report may be disclosed or used as evidence in court proceedings. This applies to financial institutions and their officers.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific data elements for suspicious transaction reports to be submitted to FIU, impacting cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions. Reports must be lodged without disclosure in court proceedings.

requirements.text: Every report under section 14 shall be lodged with the FIU
Reports must include identification of parties to the transaction
Reports must include transaction amount, nature, and circumstances raising suspicion
Reports must include business relationship with reporting person or auditor
Reports must include whether suspect is still affiliated with reporting person or auditor
Reports must include voluntary statements on origin, source, or destination of proceeds
Reports must include impact on financial soundness of reporting institution or person
Reports must include names of all officers, employees, or agents dealing with transaction
No report may be disclosed or admissible as evidence in court proceedings

sections.source_quote: Every report under section 14 shall be lodged with the FIU. For the purposes of this Part, every report shall be in such form as the FIU may approve and shall include - (a) the identification of the party or parties to the transaction; (b) the amount of the transaction, the description of the nature of the transaction and all the circumstances giving rise to the suspicion; (c) the business relationship of the suspect with the reporting person or auditor; (d) where the suspect is an insider, any information as to whether the suspect is still affiliated with the reporting person or auditor; (e) any voluntary statement as to the origin, source or destination of the proceeds; (f) the impact of the suspicious activity on the financial soundness of the reporting institution or person; and (g) the names of all the officers, employees or agents dealing with the transaction. No report of a suspicious transaction shall be required to be disclosed, or be admissible as evidence, in any court proceedings.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial reporting to FIU with specific data requirements

sections.heading: Legal consequences of reporting
sections.title: Confidentiality and legal protections for suspicious transaction reports
sections.summary: Reporting persons and auditors must maintain confidentiality of suspicious transaction reports. They cannot disclose that a report has been filed to the FIU. Supervisory authorities may request reports for compliance purposes. Reporting persons are protected from liability for good-faith disclosures. Failure to comply with confidentiality obligations constitutes an offense punishable by fines up to 5 million rupees and imprisonment up to 10 years.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides legal protections for reporting persons who submit suspicious transaction reports, ensuring confidentiality while allowing supervisory authorities to access reports for compliance purposes. This affects data sharing and reporting obligations across borders.

requirements.text: Reporting persons and auditors must not disclose that a suspicious transaction report is being filed
Supervisory authorities may request FIU to provide copies of reports for compliance functions
Reporting persons are protected from liability for good-faith reports
Reporting persons are protected from liability for good-faith information provided to FIU
Reporting persons who receive or share reports must not incur liability for confidentiality breaches
Reporting persons must have adequate safeguards for confidentiality within the group
Failure to comply with confidentiality obligations constitutes an offense punishable by fines up to 5 million rupees and imprisonment up to 10 years

sections.source_quote: Any reporting person and auditor, and any of their officers shall not disclose to any person that a suspicious transaction report is being or has been filed, or that related information is being or has been requested by, furnished or submitted to FIU. Any person who fails to comply with subsection (1) shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding 5 million rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 10 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Confidentiality of financial data transfers

sections.heading: Risk assessment
sections.title: Risk assessment and customer due diligence requirements
sections.summary: Reporting persons must conduct risk assessments for money laundering and terrorism financing. They must identify and assess risks related to customers, countries, products, services, transactions, and geographic areas. Risk assessments must consider business size, product complexity, customer relationships, third-party reliance, and technological developments. Reporting persons must document assessments and implement policies to mitigate identified risks. Enhanced due diligence is required for high-risk customers or transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires comprehensive risk assessments for money laundering and terrorism financing, impacting data transfer and processing across borders. Mandates enhanced due diligence for high-risk transactions and customers, affecting data sharing and storage practices.

requirements.text: Reporting persons must identify, assess and understand money laundering and terrorism financing risks for customers, countries, geographic areas, products, services, transactions, or delivery channels
Reporting persons must consider all relevant risk factors before determining risk levels and mitigation measures
Risk assessments must be appropriate for the business size and nature
Risk assessments must take into account all relevant risk factors including business activities, products, customers, technological developments, and national risk assessments
Reporting persons must identify and assess money laundering or terrorism financing risks for new products, business practices, or technologies
Reporting persons must document risk assessments in writing and keep them up to date
Reporting persons must establish policies, controls, and procedures to mitigate identified risks
Reporting persons must monitor, review, and update policies, controls, and procedures
Reporting persons must maintain written records of policies, controls, procedures, and changes
Reporting persons must maintain written records of communication of policies, controls, and procedures
Reporting persons must not establish or maintain anonymous or fictitious accounts
Reporting persons must undertake customer due diligence measures for new accounts, transactions above 500,000 rupees, wire transfers, or suspected money laundering
Reporting persons must apply enhanced due diligence for high-risk customers
Reporting persons may apply simplified due diligence for low-risk customers
Reporting persons must apply CDD measures for virtual asset service providers for transactions above 1,000 USD
Reporting persons must record virtual asset wallet addresses for transactions below 1,000 USD
Reporting persons must apply CDD measures for customers not physically present using reliable digital identification
Reporting persons must apply CDD measures based on risk assessment outcomes
Reporting persons must maintain records of customer transactions for 7 years
Reporting persons must maintain records of suspicious transaction reports for 7 years

sections.source_quote: Every reporting person shall take appropriate steps to identify, assess and understand the money laundering and terrorism financing risks for customers, countries or geographic areas and products, services, transactions or delivery channels; consider all relevant risk factors before determining what is the level of overall risk and the appropriate level and type of mitigation to be applied.
sections.relevance_reason: Money laundering risk assessment for financial data

sections.heading: Risk assessment (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: Risk assessment for data transfers

sections.heading: Regulatory action in the event of non-compliance
sections.title: Regulatory enforcement for non-compliance
sections.summary: Supervisory authorities may issue guidelines to combat money laundering. The Bank of Mauritius supervises banks and cash dealers, with access to all books and records. The Financial Services Commission supervises financial institutions. Banks and cash dealers failing to comply with requirements may face penalties, including administrative fines recoverable from their bank balances. Financial institutions failing to comply may be prosecuted under the Financial Services Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides regulatory enforcement mechanisms for non-compliance with data transfer and processing obligations, including access to records and potential penalties for financial institutions.
sections.authority: Bank of Mauritius, Financial Services Commission

requirements.text: Supervisory authorities may issue guidelines to combat money laundering
Bank of Mauritius supervises banks and cash dealers with access to all books and records
Financial Services Commission supervises financial institutions with access to all books and records
Bank of Mauritius may proceed against banks for non-compliance under Banking Act
Bank of Mauritius may proceed against cash dealers for non-compliance under Banking Act
Bank of Mauritius may impose administrative penalties on banks and cash dealers
Bank of Mauritius considers seriousness of breach and duration when determining penalties
Financial Services Commission may proceed against financial institutions under Financial Services Act

sections.source_quote: The Bank of Mauritius shall supervise and enforce compliance by banks and cash dealers with the requirements imposed by this Act, regulations made under this Act and such guidelines as it may issue under paragraph (a), and notwithstanding this Act, it shall, for that purpose, have access to all books, records and other information, in either physical or electronic form, maintained by banks and cash dealers.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory enforcement for financial compliance

sections.heading: Offences relating to obligation to report and keep records and to disclosure of
sections.title: Offences for failing to report, destroy records or disclose information
sections.summary: Reporting persons and their directors/employees must report breaches of reporting obligations under sections 17-17G, maintain records, and prevent false transactions. Failure to comply results in fines up to 10 million rupees and imprisonment up to 5 years. Similarly, persons who falsify information relevant to mutual assistance requests or disclose investigations into money laundering offences face fines up to 1 million rupees and imprisonment up to 5 years.
sections.dtia_summary: Fails to report, destroy records or disclose information: imposes penalties for non-compliance with reporting obligations and record-keeping, relevant to data transfer impact assessments involving financial data.

requirements.text: Reporting persons must report breaches of reporting obligations under sections 17-17G
Reporting persons must maintain records
Reporting persons must prevent false transactions
Failure to comply with reporting obligations results in fines up to 10 million rupees and imprisonment up to 5 years
Falsifying information relevant to mutual assistance requests is an offence
Disclosing investigations into money laundering offences is an offence
Failure to comply with information falsification or disclosure results in fines up to 1 million rupees and imprisonment up to 5 years

sections.source_quote: Any reporting person, or any director, employee, agent or other legal representative of a reporting person who, knowingly or without reasonable excuse – (a) fails to comply with section 17, 17A, 17B. 17C, 17D, 17E, 17F or 17G; (b) destroys or removes any record, register or document which is required under this Act or any regulations; or (c) facilitates or permits the performance under a false identity of any transaction falling within this Part, shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding 10 million rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 5 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Fines/imprisonment for breach reporting

sections.heading: Offences relating to obligation to report and keep records and to disclosure of (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: reporting obligations and record-keeping

sections.heading: Offences relating to obligation to report and keep records and to disclosure of (cont.)
sections.title: Regulatory body powers to direct compliance with financial investigations
sections.summary: Regulatory bodies may direct members to produce documents, examine records, retain evidence, or provide information relevant to financial inspections. Members must grant full access to records as needed. Failure to comply with these directions constitutes an offence punishable by fines up to 1 million rupees and imprisonment for up to 5 years. Destroying or falsifying relevant documents also incurs severe penalties.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulatory bodies can compel financial institutions to produce records and evidence during inspections. Non-compliance risks significant fines and imprisonment. This section impacts DTIAs by establishing obligations for record retention and cooperation during cross-border financial investigations.

requirements.text: Regulatory body may request assistance from FIU or supervisory authorities
Regulatory body may direct members to produce relevant documents
Regulatory body may examine and copy documents relevant to inspection
Regulatory body may retain relevant documents
Regulatory body may direct employees to provide information relevant to inspection
Member must grant full access to relevant records for inspection
Intentional obstruction of regulatory body during inspection constitutes an offence
Failure to comply with regulatory directions without reasonable excuse constitutes an offence
Destruction or falsification of relevant documents constitutes an offence

sections.source_quote: A regulatory body may, when exercising a power under subsection (1), request such assistance as may be necessary from FIU or any supervisory authorities.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory powers for financial data inspections

sections.heading: Offences relating to obligation to report and keep records and to disclosure of (cont.)
sections.title: Review panel procedures for administrative sanctions
sections.summary: The Review Panel oversees appeals against administrative sanctions imposed by regulatory bodies. It consists of a chairperson and two members with expertise in AML/CFT, law, or accountancy. The panel reviews decisions within 21 days, may suspend implementation, and ensures procedural fairness. Members must maintain confidentiality and recuse themselves if conflicts of interest exist.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a review mechanism for administrative sanctions, ensuring due process for financial institutions. It impacts DTIAs by defining procedures for challenging regulatory actions and maintaining transparency in sanction enforcement.

requirements.text: Review Panel consists of a retired judge or barrister with 15+ years' standing and two members with AML/CFT, law, or accountancy expertise
Panel members may resign with one month written notice
Panel members cease office if unfit, misconduct occurs, or breach of trust
Panel may co-opt additional members for specific reviews
Panel operates independently without external direction
Panel members must maintain confidentiality of matters
Panel members must disclose conflicts of interest before meetings
Panel members must recuse themselves from deliberations if conflicts exist
Panel members must take confidentiality oath before duties

sections.source_quote: There shall be, for the purposes of this Part, a Review Panel which shall – (a) be responsible to review a decision of a regulatory body to impose an administrative sanction under section 19N
sections.relevance_reason: Panel procedures for AML/CFT oversight

sections.heading: Membership of international financial intelligence groups and provision of
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for financial intelligence

sections.heading: Provision of information by FIU to investigatory authorities, supervisory
sections.title: FIU sharing information with supervisory authorities and investigatory bodies
sections.summary: The FIU must share information with supervisory authorities, Counterterrorism Units, Real Estate Agent Authorities, Financial Reporting Councils, or Registrars if it relates to potential money laundering, corruption, or investigations. The FIU must also share information with investigatory authorities within 48 hours if relevant to ongoing investigations. Confidentiality agreements with external bodies may require consent before sharing information. The FIU must respond to requests for suspicious transaction reports within 21 days, with possible extensions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates timely sharing of sensitive financial data with authorities, requiring DTIAs to assess data flows to supervisory bodies and investigatory authorities while ensuring confidentiality agreements are respected.

requirements.text: FIU may share information with supervisory authorities if it relates to their functions, Counterterrorism Unit, Real Estate Agent Authority, Financial Reporting Council, or Registrars
FIU must share information with investigatory authorities within 48 hours if relevant to investigations
FIU must share information with the Financial Crimes Commission for possible corruption offences
FIU may provide suspicious transaction reports to supervisory authorities for compliance purposes
FIU must respond to requests for suspicious transaction reports within 21 days, with possible 21-day extension
Information shared from outside Mauritius must have consent from the originating body

sections.source_quote: Where it becomes aware of any information, which- (a) may be relevant to the functions of any of the supervisory authorities, the Counterterrorism Unit, the Real Estate Agent Authority, the Financial Reporting Council or Registrars; and (b) does not of itself justify a dissemination to any of the investigatory authorities under section 13, FIU may, by itself or shall, at the request of the supervisory authorities the Counterterrorism Unit, the Real Estate Authority or the Financial Reporting Council or Registrars, subject to subsection (4), pass on the information to the relevant supervisory authority the Counterterrorism Unit the Real Estate Agent Authority, the Financial Reporting Council or Registrar.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data sharing for financial crime

sections.heading: Provision of information by supervisory authorities, Counterterrorism Unit, Real
sections.title: Supervisory authorities sharing information with FIU for money laundering
sections.summary: Supervisory authorities, Counterterrorism Units, Real Estate Agent Authorities, Financial Reporting Councils, and Registrars must immediately share information suggesting money laundering or suspicious transactions with the FIU. No liability exists for disclosures made under this section, and the term 'officer' includes directors, employees, agents, and legal representatives.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires immediate sharing of financial data with the FIU for money laundering investigations, necessitating DTIAs to evaluate data flows between supervisory bodies and the FIU while ensuring no liability for disclosures.

requirements.text: Supervisory authorities, Counterterrorism Units, Real Estate Agent Authorities, Financial Reporting Councils, and Registrars must share information suggesting money laundering or suspicious transactions with FIU immediately
No liability exists for disclosures made under this section by supervisory authorities or their officers
The term 'officer' includes directors, employees, agents, and legal representatives

sections.source_quote: Notwithstanding any other enactment, where, at any time in the course of the discharge of its functions, any supervisory authority, the Counterterrorism Unit, the Real Estate Authority, the Financial Reporting Council or Registrar receives, or otherwise becomes aware of, any information suggesting the possibility of a money laundering offence or suspicious transaction, the supervisory authority, the Counterterrorism Unit, the Real Estate Authority, the Financial Reporting Council or Registrar, shall, forthwith pass on that information to the FIU.
sections.relevance_reason: Money laundering data sharing with FIU

sections.heading: Money laundering offence to be extraditable
sections.title: FIU accounting and reporting requirements
sections.summary: The FIU must maintain proper accounting records for all transactions, submit a 3-year strategic plan, financial estimates, and an annual report compliant with IPSAS and corporate governance standards. The Director is responsible for timely submission of reports to the Board and Minister, with specific deadlines for each report type. Disciplinary actions may be taken for non-compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes detailed reporting and record-keeping requirements for the FIU, impacting DTIAs through data storage and retention obligations for financial records and compliance documentation.

requirements.text: FIU must keep proper accounting records for all transactions
FIU must submit a 3-year strategic plan to the Minister by 30 June each year
FIU must submit financial estimates to the Minister by 31 August each year
FIU must prepare an annual report including financial statements, activities report, and governance report
Director must submit annual report to Board within 3 months after financial year ends
Director must submit annual report to Director of Audit within 30 April after financial year ends
Director of Audit must submit annual report and audit report to Board within 6 months
Board may take disciplinary action for officers who fail to comply with reporting requirements

sections.source_quote: The FIU shall keep and maintain proper accounting records for the purpose of recording all transactions relating to its undertakings, funds, activities and property.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial reporting and record-keeping for FIU

sections.heading: Confidentiality
sections.title: Confidentiality obligations for FIU personnel
sections.summary: FIU personnel must take an oath of confidentiality before starting duties and maintain confidentiality of relevant matters. Sensitive information may only be disclosed to carry out FIU functions, prevent crime, or fulfill international obligations. Violations can result in fines up to one million rupees and imprisonment for up to three years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes strict confidentiality requirements on FIU personnel, affecting DTIAs through data sharing restrictions and disclosure protocols for sensitive financial information.

requirements.text: FIU personnel must take confidentiality oath before starting duties
FIU personnel must maintain confidentiality of relevant matters during and after employment
Sensitive information may only be disclosed to carry out FIU functions, prevent crime, or fulfill international obligations
Violations can result in fines up to one million rupees and imprisonment for up to three years

sections.source_quote: No information from which an individual or body can be identified and which is acquired by FIU in the course of carrying out its functions shall be disclosed except where the disclosure appears to FIU to be necessary - (a) to enable FIU to carry out its functions; (b) in the interests of the prevention or detection of crime; or (c) in connection with the discharge of any international obligation to which Mauritius is subject.
sections.relevance_reason: Confidentiality obligations for financial data handling

sections.heading: Declaration of assets
sections.title: Asset declaration requirements for FIU personnel
sections.summary: FIU personnel must file asset declarations with the Financial Crimes Commission within 30 days of appointment and annually thereafter. Declarations may only be disclosed with the consent of the relevant FIU official or by court order. Non-compliance can result in fines up to one million rupees and imprisonment for up to two years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires asset declarations for FIU personnel, impacting DTIAs through data sharing restrictions and disclosure protocols for personal financial information.

requirements.text: FIU personnel must file asset declarations with Financial Crimes Commission within 30 days of appointment
FIU personnel must file fresh asset declarations annually and upon employment termination
Asset declarations may only be disclosed with consent of relevant FIU official or by court order
Non-compliance can result in fines up to one million rupees and imprisonment for up to two years

sections.source_quote: The Director, every officer of FIU, and the Chairperson and every member of the Board shall file with the Financial Crimes Commission, not later than 30 days from his appointment, a declaration of his assets and liabilities in the form set out in the Third Schedule.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: personnel asset declarations not directly data protection

sections.heading: Protection from liability
sections.title: Liability protection for FIU personnel
sections.summary: No legal action can be taken against FIU, its directors, officers, or Board members for acts done in good faith while exercising functions under this Act. The Review Panel is also protected from liability for actions taken in good faith. This section provides immunity for compliance activities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section offers liability protection for FIU personnel, reducing DTIA risks by ensuring actions taken in good faith for compliance purposes are not subject to legal action.

requirements.text: No legal action can be taken against FIU, its directors, officers, or Board members for acts done in good faith while exercising functions under this Act
No legal action can be taken against the Review Panel for acts done in good faith while exercising functions under this Act

sections.source_quote: No action shall lie against FIU, the Director, any officer of FIU, or the Chairperson and members of the Board, as the case may be, in respect of any act done or omission made by FIU, the Director, any officer of the FIU, or the Chairperson or members of the Board, as the case may be, in good faith, in the exercise of the functions conferred on FIU under this Act or any other enactment.
sections.relevance_reason: Liability protection for compliance roles

sections.heading: Funding
sections.title: Funding sources for Financial Intelligence Unit
sections.summary: The Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) expenses are funded annually by Parliament appropriations and government grants. FIU may accept donations with Minister's consent, and Article 910 of the Code Civil Mauricien does not apply to donations to FIU.
sections.dtia_summary: FIU funding mechanisms ensure financial intelligence operations are adequately resourced without conflicting with international obligations.

requirements.text: FIU expenses are met from annual Parliament appropriations
FIU expenses are met from government grants
FIU may accept donations with Minister's consent
Article 910 of the Code Civil Mauricien does not apply to donations to FIU

sections.source_quote: The expenses of FIU shall be met out of - (a) monies appropriated annually by Parliament for the purposes of FIU; and (b) any Government grants made to it.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for financial data handling

sections.heading: Regulations
sections.title: Regulations for money laundering prevention
sections.summary: The Minister may make regulations to implement this Act, including provisions to enable Mauritius to comply with international obligations related to money laundering, terrorism financing, and proliferation financing. Regulations may impose fines up to one million rupees and imprisonment for up to five years for contraventions.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulations establish enforcement mechanisms for cross-border financial crime prevention, including potential penalties for non-compliance with international obligations.

requirements.text: Minister may make regulations for the purposes of this Act
Regulations may enable Mauritius to comply with international obligations on money laundering, terrorism financing, and proliferation financing
Regulations may provide for offences with fines up to one million rupees and imprisonment up to five years
Regulations may provide for amendment of Schedules

sections.source_quote: The Minister may make such regulations as he thinks fit for the purposes of this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulations for AML compliance with penalties

sections.heading: Consequential amendments
sections.title: Consequential amendments to financial legislation
sections.summary: This section amends the Banking Act, Financial Services Act 2001, and Foreign Exchange Dealers Act to replace references to older money laundering legislation with the Financial Intelligence and Anti-Money Laundering Act 2002. The amendments update the legal framework for financial crime prevention.
sections.dtia_summary: Amendments ensure alignment of financial legislation with the latest anti-money laundering requirements, facilitating cross-border financial crime detection.

requirements.text: Banking Act amended to include terrorism offences under Prevention of Terrorism Act 2002
Banking Act amended to include money laundering under Financial Intelligence and Anti-Money Laundering Act 2002
Financial Services Act 2001 amended to replace Economic Crime and Anti-Money Laundering Act 2000 references
Foreign Exchange Dealers Act amended to replace Economic Crime and Anti-Money Laundering Act 2000 references

sections.source_quote: The Banking Act is amended - (a) in section 39A(3) - (i) by inserting immediately after the words "arms trafficking", the words ",offences related to terrorism under the Prevention of Terrorism Act 2002"
sections.relevance_reason: AML legislation updates affect financial data handling

sections.heading: Professional
sections.title: Professional accountants under Financial Reporting Act
sections.summary: Professional accountants and public accountants under the Financial Reporting Act are defined as sole practitioners, partners, or employed professionals within member firms. This section specifies the scope of professionals covered by the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Clarifies which professionals are subject to financial intelligence requirements, ensuring accurate identification of entities needing compliance.

requirements.text: Professional accountants and public accountants under Financial Reporting Act are sole practitioners, partners, or employed professionals within member firms

sections.source_quote: Professional accountant, and public accountant under the Financial Reporting Act only where they are sole practitioners, partners or employed professionals within member firms
sections.relevance_reason: Pure definition of financial professionals, no data compliance relevance

sections.heading: Person licensed to operate a casino, a hotel
sections.title: Casino and hotel operators under Gambling Act
sections.summary: Individuals licensed to operate casinos, hotels, horse racing, lotteries, gaming houses, and other gambling-related activities are defined under the Gambling Regulatory Authority Act. This section specifies the scope of gambling operators subject to financial intelligence requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines gambling operators subject to financial intelligence monitoring, particularly those handling transactions above 20,000 rupees.

requirements.text: Person licensed to operate casino, hotel, horse racing, lottery, gaming house, gaming machine, etc. under Gambling Regulatory Authority Act

sections.source_quote: Person licensed to operate a casino, a hotel casino, as a horse racing organiser, the Mauritius National Lottery, a limited payout machine, a sweepstake, as a local pool promoter, as the agent of a local pool promoter, a gaming house, a gaming machine, as a totalisator, as a bookmaker and interactive gambling under the Gambling Regulatory Authority Act
sections.relevance_reason: Gambling operators' financial data compliance

sections.heading: Company Service Provider
sections.title: Company Service Providers
sections.summary: Company Service Providers are defined as registrars of companies. This section specifies the scope of entities that may be subject to financial intelligence requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Identifies company service providers as entities that may require financial intelligence monitoring, particularly in relation to company formation and management.

requirements.text: Company Service Provider is defined as Registrar of Companies

sections.source_quote: Company Service Provider Registrar of Companies
sections.relevance_reason: Defines entities subject to financial intelligence requirements

sections.heading: My assets and those of my spouse and minor children (extent and nature of interests
sections.title: Asset declaration for individuals and family members
sections.summary: This section requires individuals to disclose their assets and those of their spouse and minor children, including immovable property, motor vehicles, partnerships, securities, cash, jewellery, and other assets exceeding 50,000 rupees. The disclosure covers both assets within Mauritius and outside the country. The declaration must be completed with specific details for each asset category.

requirements.text: Disclose immovable property (freehold and leasehold) in Mauritius and outside Mauritius
Disclose motor vehicles
Disclose interest in partnerships, societies, joint ventures, or succession
Disclose securities including treasury bills and units
Disclose cash in bank
Disclose cash in hand exceeding 50,000 rupees
Disclose jewellery and precious metals
Disclose other assets exceeding 50,000 rupees in aggregate (specify)

sections.source_quote: My assets and those of my spouse and minor children (extent and nature of interests therein) in Mauritius and outside Mauritius are as follows - (a) immovable property – (i) freehold …………………………………………………………………………. (ii) leasehold ……………………………………………………………………….. (b) motor vehicles ………………………………………………………………………….. (c) interest in any partnership, société, joint venture or succession …………………. (d) securities including treasury bills, units, etc. ……………………………………….. (e) cash in bank …………………………………………………………………………… (f) cash in hand exceeding 50,000 rupees ……………………………………………….. (g) jewellery and precious metals ………………………………………………………. (h) other assets exceeding 50,000 rupees in the aggregate (specify) ………………
sections.relevance_reason: Pure asset declaration, no data protection/privacy focus

sections.heading: My liabilities and those of my spouse and minor children are as follows -
sections.title: Liability declaration for individuals and family members
sections.summary: This section requires individuals to disclose their liabilities and those of their spouse and minor children. The declaration must be completed with specific details of liabilities, followed by a signature and affirmation before a Master and Registrar of the Supreme Court. The declaration is part of a legal process for asset and liability reporting.

requirements.text: Disclose liabilities and those of spouse and minor children

sections.source_quote: My liabilities and those of my spouse and minor children are as follows - ………………………………………………………………………………………………… …………………………………………………………………………………… Signature Sworn/solemnly affirmed by the abovenamed before me at this………... day of……………....
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance
